Algorithm: AES-256-GCM
Category: symmetric
Status: NIST Standard
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: General-purpose authenticated encryption. TLS 1.3, disk encryption, government classified data.
Why Recommended: NIST-standardized, ubiquitous hardware acceleration via AES-NI, proven secure under PRP assumption, and deployed as default in TLS 1.3.
Caution: Nonce reuse is catastrophic. If your system cannot guarantee unique nonces, use XChaCha20-Poly1305 or AES-GCM-SIV instead. Requires AES-NI for timing-safe implementation.

Algorithm: ChaCha20-Poly1305
Category: symmetric
Status: IETF RFC 8439
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Authenticated encryption without AES hardware. Mobile, TLS 1.3 fallback, WireGuard.
Why Recommended: Constant-time by construction with no lookup tables, eliminating cache-timing attacks. IETF-standardized (RFC 8439), proven Poly1305 MAC, and default cipher in WireGuard and TLS 1.3 fallback.
Caution: Slightly slower than AES-256-GCM with AES-NI. 96-bit nonce limits safe random generation to ~2^32 messages per key. Use XChaCha20 for high-volume random nonces.

Algorithm: XChaCha20-Poly1305
Category: symmetric
Status: IETF Draft / libsodium
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: File/at-rest encryption needing random nonces safely. libsodium default.
Why Recommended: Extends ChaCha20-Poly1305 with a 192-bit nonce, making random nonce generation safe for high-volume encryption without birthday concerns. Default in libsodium. Inherits all security properties of ChaCha20-Poly1305.
Caution: Not a finalized IETF RFC (draft status). Less ecosystem tooling in some frameworks. Use ChaCha20-Poly1305 if 96-bit nonces are sufficient.

Algorithm: Camellia-256
Category: symmetric
Status: CRYPTREC / ISO / IETF
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Japanese government, TLS suites, NESSIE-approved applications.
Why Recommended: Approved by CRYPTREC (Japan), NESSIE (EU), and ISO/IEC 18033-3. Comparable security margin to AES after 20+ years of cryptanalysis. Suitable where AES is not mandated or regional compliance requires alternatives.
Caution: No hardware acceleration equivalent to AES-NI, 2-3x slower in software. Limited ecosystem support outside Japan/EU compliance contexts.

Algorithm: ARIA-256
Category: symmetric
Status: Korean Standard KS X 1213
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Korean government and financial systems.
Why Recommended: Korean national standard (KS X 1213) mandated for government use. SPN structure analyzed for 15+ years with no practical attacks. Acceptable where Korean compliance is required.
Caution: No dedicated hardware acceleration, 3-5x slower than AES-NI. Limited deployment outside Korean government systems.

Algorithm: SM4
Category: symmetric
Status: GB/T 32907 / ISO 18033-3
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 64
Use Cases: Chinese government, banking, wireless LAN (WAPI).
Why Recommended: Chinese national standard (GB/T 32907) required for government and financial systems. Open specification with ISO endorsement (ISO/IEC 18033-3:2010). Only 128-bit key limits post-quantum security.
Caution: Only 128-bit key means post-quantum security is marginal at 64-bit (Grover). No hardware acceleration outside Chinese platforms.

Algorithm: Kuznyechik
Category: symmetric
Status: GOST R 34.12-2015
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Russian government and military systems.
Why Recommended: Russian national standard (GOST R 34.12-2015) required for government systems. Functionally sound block cipher, but undisclosed S-box derivation has drawn international scrutiny regarding design transparency.
Caution: Undisclosed S-box derivation raises transparency concerns. Cannot verify nothing-up-my-sleeve property. Limited international cryptanalysis.

Algorithm: SNOW-V
Category: symmetric
Status: 3GPP / ISO/IEC 18033-4
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: 5G confidentiality and integrity (3GPP). High-throughput stream encryption. Successor to SNOW 3G used in 4G/LTE.
Why Recommended: SNOW 2.0 is ISO/IEC 18033-4 standardized and SNOW 3G is the 3GPP stream cipher for 4G/LTE. SNOW-V is the 256-bit successor designed for 5G throughput. Proven LFSR + FSM construction with 20+ years of cryptanalysis on the family.
Caution: Stream cipher — no built-in authentication (must pair with a MAC). Less ecosystem support outside telecom. Not a drop-in replacement for AES-GCM in general-purpose AEAD.

Algorithm: AES-256-GCM-SIV
Category: symmetric
Status: IETF RFC 8452
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Nonce-misuse-resistant authenticated encryption. Systems where nonce uniqueness cannot be guaranteed — distributed systems, cloud KMS, at-rest encryption.
Why Recommended: RFC 8452 standardized. Provides nonce-misuse resistance via synthetic IV construction — nonce reuse leaks only whether two plaintexts are identical, without catastrophic key/auth compromise. Used by Google Tink, AWS S3, and cloud KMS systems. AES-NI hardware acceleration.
Caution: Slightly slower than AES-GCM (~2x due to POLYVAL + SIV construction). Not a drop-in replacement — different API semantics. If nonce uniqueness is guaranteed, AES-GCM is faster.

Algorithm: Curve25519 / X25519
Category: curve
Status: RFC 7748 / deployed
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Modern elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman, Signal/WireGuard-style key agreement, the base curve behind the 25519 ecosystem.
Why Recommended: Modern rigid curve design with simple constant-time ladders, strong deployment maturity, and a long track record in TLS, Signal, WireGuard, and libsodium. Recommended default for classical elliptic-curve key agreement when a curve is still required.
Caution: Not post-quantum safe — Shor's algorithm breaks ECDLP in polynomial time on a sufficiently large quantum computer. Migrate to ML-KEM-768 for key encapsulation or use X25519+ML-KEM-768 hybrid while transitioning.

Algorithm: Ed25519
Category: curve
Status: RFC 8032
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Modern deterministic signatures, SSH keys, software signing, threshold-signature systems such as FROST.
Why Recommended: Best current default for classical curve-based signatures thanks to simple deterministic signing, complete Edwards formulas, broad library support, and mature deployment in SSH, Git, package signing, and threshold-signature systems.
Caution: Not post-quantum safe — Shor's algorithm breaks EdDSA in polynomial time. Migrate to ML-DSA-44 for signatures or maintain Ed25519+ML-DSA-44 hybrid during transition. Do not use for long-term signing keys beyond the quantum transition horizon.

Algorithm: P-256
Category: curve
Status: NIST SP 800-186 / FIPS 186-5
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: NIST-required government and enterprise ECC, ECDSA, ECDH, TLS certificates, hardware tokens, PIV and smart-card ecosystems.
Why Recommended: 128-bit classical security and unmatched deployment depth in compliance-driven environments. Acceptable where NIST profiles, FIPS modules, smart cards, or PKI ecosystems require it.
Caution: Not quantum-safe, and the NIST P-curves attract ongoing debate about rigidity and implementation complexity. If you are not constrained by compliance, modern 25519-family curves are often easier to use safely.

Algorithm: P-384
Category: curve
Status: NIST SP 800-186 / FIPS 186-5
Security Bits: 192
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Higher-assurance classical ECC, government and enterprise PKI, long-lived roots, and environments targeting a 192-bit classical security tier.
Why Recommended: NIST-assigned 192-bit classical security with deep standards support. Acceptable for higher-assurance classical deployments that must remain inside the NIST curve family.
Caution: Not post-quantum safe — Shor's algorithm breaks ECDLP regardless of curve size. Larger key size provides no quantum protection. Migrate to ML-KEM or ML-DSA as appropriate.

Algorithm: P-521
Category: curve
Status: NIST SP 800-186 / FIPS 186-5
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Very high classical-assurance ECC, specialized PKI roots, and conservative long-lived classical deployments.
Why Recommended: NIST maps P-521 into the 256-bit classical security tier. Acceptable for organizations that explicitly want the largest standardized NIST prime curve and can tolerate the implementation and interoperability cost.
Caution: Large and slower, with limited ecosystem enthusiasm compared with P-256 or P-384, and still completely broken by Shor's algorithm. The extra classical security margin provides zero quantum protection. Use ML-KEM or ML-DSA for post-quantum deployments.

Algorithm: secp256k1
Category: curve
Status: SEC 2 / blockchain deployed
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Bitcoin and Ethereum signatures, blockchain wallets, threshold ECDSA, and cryptocurrency custody infrastructure.
Why Recommended: Acceptable only where blockchain compatibility is non-negotiable. It is deeply entrenched in Bitcoin and Ethereum tooling, wallets, hardware devices, and threshold-ECDSA custody systems.
Caution: Not post-quantum safe, not the easiest curve for general-purpose software engineering, and largely justified by ecosystem lock-in rather than by being the best modern default.

Algorithm: Ed448 / Curve448
Category: curve
Status: RFC 7748 / RFC 8032
Security Bits: 224
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Higher-security classical signatures and key agreement, niche high-assurance deployments, and ecosystems standardizing on the 448-bit family.
Why Recommended: Provides a higher classical target than the 25519 family while retaining modern curve-design principles. Acceptable for higher-assurance classical deployments that want modern CFRG-style curves instead of larger NIST curves.
Caution: Less deployed than the 25519 family, larger and slower, and still fully broken by Shor's algorithm. No quantum protection from the larger key size. Migrate to post-quantum alternatives for long-term deployments.

Algorithm: BLS12-381
Category: curve
Status: IETF draft / RFC 9380 suites
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: BLS signatures, signature aggregation, KZG commitments, Ethereum consensus, zk systems, and pairing-based cryptographic constructions.
Why Recommended: Acceptable for pairing-heavy systems that specifically need aggregation, KZG commitments, or zk proof ecosystems already standardized around BLS12-381. It is the current practical default among modern pairing-friendly curves with an approximately 128-bit target.
Caution: Pairings are specialized, expensive, and not quantum-safe. Both the discrete log and pairing assumptions are broken by Shor's algorithm. Outside aggregation, commitment, and zk use cases, BLS12-381 adds complexity without benefit. No post-quantum equivalent exists for pairing-based constructions today.

Algorithm: BN254
Category: curve
Status: Academic / zkSNARK deployed
Security Bits: 100
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Legacy zk-SNARK circuits, Ethereum precompiles, Groth16-era proving systems, and ecosystems locked to alt_bn128 compatibility.
Why Recommended: Acceptable only for compatibility with established zk-SNARK circuits and Ethereum ecosystems that still depend on BN254 or alt_bn128. It remains operationally important, but only inside those legacy proof stacks.
Caution: Security margin is materially weaker than the old 128-bit folklore estimate — Kim-Barbulescu attacks reduce effective security to approximately 100 bits. Fully broken by Shor's algorithm. Do not use for new zk systems — prefer BLS12-381 or a post-quantum proof system.

Algorithm: Brainpool P-256r1
Category: curve
Status: RFC 5639 / BSI
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: EU and German government profiles, compliance-driven ECC, smart-card and PKI stacks that prefer Brainpool domain parameters.
Why Recommended: Acceptable in European and German compliance contexts that explicitly call for Brainpool parameters. It offers the conventional 128-bit security tier while satisfying region-specific policy requirements.
Caution: Less common tooling than P-256 or the 25519 family, and still fully broken by Shor's algorithm. BSI has published post-quantum migration guidance — plan migration to ML-KEM and ML-DSA for long-term deployments.

Algorithm: ML-KEM-768 (Kyber)
Category: kem
Status: NIST FIPS 203
Security Bits: 192
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 192
Use Cases: PQ key encapsulation. Hybrid TLS (Chrome, Firefox). Recommended default.
Why Recommended: NIST primary PQ KEM standard (FIPS 203), already deployed in Chrome and Firefox hybrid TLS, tight reduction to Module-LWE with strong security margin.
Caution: Larger key and ciphertext sizes than classical ECDH (~1.2 KB vs ~32 bytes). If lattice problems are broken, all ML-KEM parameter sets fail. Diversify with HQC.

Algorithm: ML-KEM-1024 (Kyber)
Category: kem
Status: NIST FIPS 203
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: PQ KEM at highest security. Government, long-term secrets.
Why Recommended: Highest security parameter set of NIST FIPS 203 (ML-KEM). Provides 256-bit classical and PQ security. Conservative choice for long-term government secrets and maximum security margin.
Caution: Even larger sizes than ML-KEM-768 with diminishing returns for most applications. ML-KEM-768 provides sufficient security for all but the most conservative long-term requirements.

Algorithm: SMAUG-T
Category: kem
Status: KPQC Round 2
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Korean PQC KEM candidate. KPQC standardization track.
Why Recommended: Korean PQC candidate in KPQC Round 2. Novel NTRU/LWE hybrid approach under active analysis. Not yet standardized — appropriate for research and experimental deployment only.
Caution: Not standardized and no independent security proof maturity. Novel NTRU/LWE hybrid assumption lacks extensive cryptanalysis. Do not use in production.

Algorithm: HQC
Category: kem
Status: NIST Selected
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: PQ KEM with code-based assumption. Diversifies away from lattice dependence.
Why Recommended: NIST-selected PQ KEM based on code-based cryptography, providing assumption diversity from lattice-based schemes. If lattice problems are broken, HQC remains secure.
Caution: 2-5x larger keys and ciphertexts than ML-KEM. Slower operations. Use ML-KEM as primary KEM; reserve HQC for assumption diversity in hybrid deployments.

Algorithm: Classic McEliece
Category: kem
Status: NIST Round 4
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Ultra-conservative PQ. When long-term security outweighs key size.
Why Recommended: Based on McEliece (1978) — oldest PQ assumption with 45+ years of cryptanalysis and no significant weakening. Enormous public keys (~261 KB) but tiny ciphertexts. Maximum confidence in security assumption.
Caution: Public keys are ~261 KB, impractical for bandwidth-constrained protocols like TLS handshakes. Key generation is slow.

Algorithm: FrodoKEM-976
Category: kem
Status: BSI Recommended
Security Bits: 192
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 192
Use Cases: Conservative PQ KEM when ring/module structure is too risky.
Why Recommended: Based on plain LWE without ring or module structure, providing more conservative security assumptions than ML-KEM. BSI (German Federal Office) recommended. Large keys trade bandwidth for assumption strength.
Caution: Public keys ~15 KB, much larger than ML-KEM. Slower operations. Use only when ring/module structure is considered too risky.

Algorithm: BIKE
Category: kem
Status: NIST Round 4
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Lightweight PQ KEM with moderate sizes.
Why Recommended: Compact code-based KEM in NIST Round 4 with small key and ciphertext sizes compared to other code-based schemes. Decapsulation has a small failure probability that must be handled correctly.
Caution: Not yet standardized by NIST. Small decapsulation failure probability requires careful handling. Use ML-KEM or HQC for standardized deployments.

Algorithm: ML-DSA-44 (Dilithium)
Category: signature
Status: NIST FIPS 204
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: PQ signatures. Certificate, firmware, document signing.
Why Recommended: NIST primary PQ signature standard (FIPS 204). Compact signatures, fast signing and verification. Recommended default for post-quantum digital signatures in most applications.
Caution: Larger signatures (~2.4 KB) than classical ECDSA (~64 bytes). If lattice problems break, all ML-DSA parameter sets fail. Pair with SLH-DSA for assumption diversity.

Algorithm: ML-DSA-65 (Dilithium)
Category: signature
Status: NIST FIPS 204
Security Bits: 192
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 192
Use Cases: PQ signatures at 192-bit security. Recommended default.
Why Recommended: Mid-security parameter of NIST FIPS 204 (ML-DSA). Balances 192-bit security level with moderate signature and key sizes. Suitable for applications requiring higher assurance than ML-DSA-44.
Caution: Larger signatures and keys than ML-DSA-44 with NIST Level 3 security that most applications do not require. Use ML-DSA-44 unless 192-bit security is explicitly needed.

Algorithm: HAETAE
Category: signature
Status: KPQC Round 2
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Korean PQC signature candidate. KPQC standardization track.
Why Recommended: Korean PQC signature candidate in KPQC Round 2. Novel lattice-based signature construction under active analysis. Not yet standardized — suitable for research and experimental use only.
Caution: Not standardized. Novel construction without independent cryptanalysis maturity. Do not use in production or certificate chains.

Algorithm: FALCON-512
Category: signature
Status: NIST (FIPS 206 pending)
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: PQ signatures where small size matters. Constrained devices, blockchain.
Why Recommended: NIST-selected PQ signature with the most compact signatures among lattice-based schemes. Uses NTRU lattices with fast Fourier sampling. Complex implementation requires constant-time floating-point arithmetic.
Caution: Complex implementation requiring constant-time floating-point arithmetic. Implementation errors have led to real-world key recovery. Use ML-DSA unless compact signatures are critical.

Algorithm: SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+)
Category: signature
Status: NIST FIPS 205
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Ultra-conservative PQ signatures. Fallback if lattice assumptions fail.
Why Recommended: NIST FIPS 205 stateless hash-based signature. Security relies only on hash function properties, not lattice or number-theoretic assumptions. Conservative fallback if lattice-based assumptions are ever broken.
Caution: Signatures are 7-49 KB depending on parameter set, much larger than ML-DSA (~2.4 KB). Slower signing. Use only as a hedge against lattice assumption failure.

Algorithm: XMSS
Category: signature
Status: IETF RFC 8391 / NIST SP 800-208
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Firmware/code signing. Stateful — needs careful state management.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-208 standardized hash-based signature. Stateful: each key can sign a limited number of messages. Minimal cryptographic assumptions — security relies only on hash function properties.
Caution: Stateful: signing key state must never be reused. State management failure causes complete security loss. Use SLH-DSA (stateless) unless NIST SP 800-208 compliance is required.

Algorithm: LMS / HSS
Category: signature
Status: NIST SP 800-208 / RFC 8554
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Firmware signing, code signing, CNSA 2.0 compliance. Stateful — designed for limited-use signing keys with predictable lifetimes.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-208 standardized alongside XMSS. Levinshtein Merkle Signature scheme with Hierarchical Signature System (HSS) for multi-tree key management. W-OTS+ one-time signatures. Simpler implementation than XMSS with comparable security guarantees.
Caution: Stateful: each leaf key can sign exactly once — reuse is catastrophic and produces real forgeries. Requires reliable persistent state tracking. Use SLH-DSA (stateless) unless firmware/code signing with predictable key lifetimes.

Algorithm: SHA-256
Category: hash
Status: NIST FIPS 180-4
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: General-purpose. TLS, Git, Bitcoin, HMAC.
Why Recommended: NIST-standardized since 2001, no practical attacks after 25+ years of cryptanalysis, hardware-accelerated via SHA-NI, and the most widely deployed hash function.
Caution: Vulnerable to length extension attacks when used directly for authentication. Use HMAC-SHA-256 or SHA-3 instead. 128-bit collision resistance may be marginal for very long-term applications.

Algorithm: SHA-512
Category: hash
Status: NIST FIPS 180-4
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Maximum SHA-2 security. Used internally by Ed25519.
Why Recommended: NIST FIPS 180-4 standard with 512-bit output and 256-bit collision resistance. Same SHA-2 family as SHA-256 with wider security margin. Preferred when 256-bit collision resistance is needed.
Caution: Slower than SHA-256 on 32-bit platforms. 512-bit output is larger than needed for most applications. Use SHA-256 unless 256-bit collision resistance is required.

Algorithm: SHA-3-256 (Keccak)
Category: hash
Status: NIST FIPS 202
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Algorithm diversity. Ethereum, PQ constructions.
Why Recommended: NIST FIPS 202 standard based on Keccak sponge construction. Provides structural diversity from SHA-2 family. Not vulnerable to length extension attacks. Recommended where SHA-2 fallback diversity is needed.
Caution: Generally slower than SHA-256 in software without hardware support. Less ecosystem tooling. Use SHA-256 for compatibility unless length-extension resistance or SHA-2 diversity is needed.

Algorithm: BLAKE2b
Category: hash
Status: IETF RFC 7693
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: High-speed hashing. Argon2 internally, libsodium, WireGuard.
Why Recommended: IETF RFC 7693 standardized. Faster than SHA-256 in software while providing equivalent security margins. Widely deployed in cryptocurrency, file integrity, and password hashing applications.
Caution: Not NIST-standardized and may not satisfy compliance requirements. No dedicated hardware acceleration. Use SHA-256 for compliance or SHA-3 for NIST-standardized diversity.

Algorithm: BLAKE3
Category: hash
Status: Published spec
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Ultra-fast hashing. File integrity, content addressing, parallel workloads.
Why Recommended: Latest in the BLAKE family with tree hashing parallelism. Extremely fast in software (3-4x BLAKE2b). Rapidly gaining deployment but not yet an IETF or NIST standard.
Caution: Reduced round count (7 vs 12 for BLAKE2b) trades security margin for speed. Not formally standardized. Security analysis is younger.

Algorithm: SM3
Category: hash
Status: GB/T 32905 / ISO 10118-3
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Chinese national hash. Digital signatures (SM2), government, banking.
Why Recommended: Chinese national standard (GB/T 32905-2016) based on Merkle-Damgård construction. Required for Chinese government and financial cryptography. Recognized by ISO/IEC 10118-3.
Caution: Limited international cryptanalysis compared to SHA-2. Design documentation less publicly available. Use SHA-256 unless Chinese compliance is mandatory.

Algorithm: Streebog
Category: hash
Status: GOST R 34.11-2012
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Russian government. GOST-mandated hashing.
Why Recommended: Russian national standard (GOST R 34.11-2012). Required for Russian government cryptographic systems. Unique Miyaguchi-Preneel-based construction with 512-bit internal state.
Caution: S-box transparency concerns (shared with Kuznyechik). Limited independent analysis. Use SHA-256 or SHA-3 outside Russian compliance contexts.

Algorithm: Kupyna-256
Category: hash
Status: DSTU 7564:2014
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Ukrainian government and digital signatures.
Why Recommended: Ukrainian national standard (DSTU 7564:2014). Based on AES-like (Rijndael) round functions adapted for hashing. Required for Ukrainian government applications.
Caution: Very limited deployment and cryptanalysis outside Ukraine. Narrow ecosystem support. Use SHA-256 unless Ukrainian compliance is required.

Algorithm: HKDF
Category: kdf
Status: IETF RFC 5869
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Deriving multiple keys from a shared secret. TLS 1.3, Signal, Noise Protocol, Quantum Vault KPQC.
Why Recommended: IETF RFC 5869 standard for extracting and expanding keying material from non-uniform sources. Used in TLS 1.3 key schedule. HMAC-based with provable security in the random oracle model.
Caution: Not suitable for password-based key derivation and not memory-hard. Requires input keying material with sufficient min-entropy. Use Argon2id for password-derived keys.

Algorithm: Argon2 (KDF mode)
Category: kdf
Status: IETF RFC 9106
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Deriving keys from passwords or low-entropy inputs. Password-based encryption.
Why Recommended: Password Hashing Competition winner (2015) used in KDF mode. Memory-hard key derivation suitable for deriving keys from passwords or low-entropy inputs with tunable cost parameters.
Caution: More complex parameter tuning than HKDF. Not suitable for high-entropy keying material where HKDF is simpler and faster.

Algorithm: scrypt
Category: kdf
Status: IETF RFC 7914
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Password-based key derivation. Cryptocurrency proof-of-work (Litecoin), file encryption.
Why Recommended: Memory-hard KDF predating Argon2. IETF RFC 7914 standardized. Suitable for key derivation from passwords with tunable memory and CPU costs, though less flexible than Argon2.
Caution: Less flexible parameter tuning than Argon2 (single cost parameter). Use Argon2id for new deployments unless scrypt is already established in your system.

Algorithm: PBKDF2
Category: kdf
Status: NIST SP 800-132
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Legacy password-based key derivation. WPA2 WiFi, LUKS disk encryption, PKCS#12.
Why Recommended: Legacy KDF standardized in NIST SP 800-132 and RFC 8018. Not memory-hard — vulnerable to GPU and ASIC acceleration. Retained only for backward compatibility with existing deployed systems.
Caution: Not memory-hard and vulnerable to GPU/ASIC parallelization. Should not be used for new deployments. Migrate to Argon2id.

Algorithm: Balloon Hashing
Category: kdf
Status: Academic / NIST consideration
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Provably memory-hard key derivation. Research-oriented, NIST consideration.
Why Recommended: Academic memory-hard KDF with provable memory-hardness guarantees (not just assumed like scrypt). Simpler security analysis but significantly less deployed than Argon2 or scrypt.
Caution: Limited deployment and library support. Not standardized. Use Argon2id for production password-derived key generation.

Algorithm: HMAC-SHA-256
Category: mac
Status: NIST FIPS 198-1 / IETF RFC 2104
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: General-purpose message authentication. TLS, API authentication, JWT signing, HKDF foundation.
Why Recommended: IETF RFC 2104 and NIST FIPS 198-1 standard. Provably secure as a PRF under standard assumptions on SHA-256 compression function. Ubiquitous in TLS, JWT, SSH, and API authentication.
Caution: Requires a hash function as component. Use KMAC if SHA-3 hardware is available and Keccak-based construction is preferred.

Algorithm: CMAC-AES-256
Category: mac
Status: NIST SP 800-38B
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Block cipher-based MAC. Payment systems (EMV), low-level protocols where AES is already available.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-38B standard deterministic MAC based on AES. Suitable when AES hardware acceleration is available and a nonce-free MAC is needed for message authentication.
Caution: Birthday-bound collision limit at 64-bit for 128-bit blocks limits safe message volume to ~2^64 blocks per key. Use HMAC-SHA-256 for higher collision resistance margin.

Algorithm: KMAC256
Category: mac
Status: NIST SP 800-185
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Keccak-native MAC. PQ constructions, systems already using SHA-3.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-185 standard based on Keccak/SHA-3. Customizable MAC with built-in domain separation. Avoids the birthday-bound collision limitation of CMAC.
Caution: Slower than HMAC-SHA-256 in software without SHA-3 hardware. Less deployment history. Use HMAC-SHA-256 unless domain separation or SHA-3 ecosystem is required.

Algorithm: Poly1305
Category: mac
Status: IETF RFC 8439
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Fast one-time MAC. Paired with ChaCha20 or AES for AEAD constructions.
Why Recommended: Information-theoretically secure one-time authenticator used as the MAC component in ChaCha20-Poly1305 and AES-GCM-SIV. Provably secure with fresh key per message.
Caution: One-time use only: key reuse with two messages reveals the authentication key. Never use standalone; always as part of an AEAD construction.

Algorithm: SipHash
Category: mac
Status: Published spec / widely deployed
Security Bits: 64
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 64
Use Cases: Hash table protection against HashDoS. Short-input MAC. Rust default HashMap, Python dict, Linux kernel.
Why Recommended: Designed for short-input authentication and hash table keying. Fast on short messages with PRF guarantees. Not a general-purpose MAC for arbitrary-length data authentication.
Caution: Not a general-purpose MAC and designed for short inputs and hash table keying. 64-bit tag variant has insufficient collision resistance for security-critical authentication.

Algorithm: Argon2id
Category: password
Status: IETF RFC 9106 / OWASP recommended
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Password storage. Modern web apps, credential databases, login systems.
Why Recommended: Password Hashing Competition winner (2015). Combines data-dependent (Argon2d) and data-independent (Argon2i) memory access for both side-channel resistance and GPU hardness. OWASP and IETF RFC 9106 recommended default.
Caution: More complex parameter tuning than bcrypt. Some legacy systems and compliance frameworks may not support it yet. Ensure library availability before committing.

Algorithm: bcrypt
Category: password
Status: OpenBSD / OWASP acceptable
Security Bits: 184
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 184
Use Cases: Password storage. Legacy but still widely deployed and acceptable.
Why Recommended: Battle-tested since 1999 with adaptive cost factor. Widely deployed and well-understood. Limited to 72-byte passwords and 128-bit internal state, which constrains future security scaling.
Caution: 72-byte password truncation silently reduces security for long passwords. 128-bit internal state limits future scaling. Use Argon2id for all new systems.

Algorithm: scrypt
Category: password
Status: IETF RFC 7914
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Password storage with memory-hard protection. Cryptocurrency, file encryption systems.
Why Recommended: Memory-hard password hashing predating Argon2. IETF RFC 7914 standardized. Suitable where Argon2 is unavailable and memory-hardness is required for password storage.
Caution: Less flexible than Argon2id with single cost parameter controlling both memory and CPU. Large memory requirements may be problematic on constrained devices.

Algorithm: PBKDF2
Category: password
Status: NIST SP 800-132 (legacy)
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Legacy. WPA2, LUKS, older systems. Being replaced everywhere.
Why Recommended: Legacy password hashing from NIST SP 800-132 and RFC 8018. Not memory-hard — highly parallelizable on GPUs and ASICs. Retained only for backward compatibility with existing systems.
Caution: Not memory-hard and GPU attacks can test billions of passwords per second. Should not be used for new systems. Migrate existing deployments to Argon2id.

Algorithm: Balloon Hashing
Category: password
Status: Academic
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Provably memory-hard password hashing. Research-stage.
Why Recommended: Academic password hashing with provable memory-hardness guarantees. Simpler security analysis than scrypt but limited real-world deployment, tooling, and library support.
Caution: Limited library support and deployment experience. Not standardized. Use Argon2id for production password hashing.

Algorithm: Shamir
Category: sharing
Status: Foundational / widely deployed
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Threshold key recovery. Cryptocurrency wallets, backup systems, Quantum Vault KPQC.
Why Recommended: Information-theoretically secure (t,n)-threshold secret sharing requiring no computational assumptions. Foundation of most modern secret sharing and MPC protocols. Deployed since 1979.
Caution: No verifiability: a malicious dealer can distribute inconsistent shares undetectably. Use Feldman or Pedersen VSS when share verification is needed.

Algorithm: Blakley
Category: sharing
Status: Foundational
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Alternative threshold sharing via hyperplane intersection. Less common than Shamir.
Why Recommended: Legacy geometric secret sharing using hyperplane intersection. Information-theoretically secure like Shamir. Less space-efficient than Shamir — shares are larger than the secret. Retained only for backward compatibility.
Caution: Shares are larger than the secret, making it less efficient than Shamir with no security advantage. Use Shamir Secret Sharing for all new threshold sharing deployments. Blakley has no practical use case where Shamir is not the better choice.

Algorithm: Feldman VSS
Category: sharing
Status: Foundational
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Verifiable secret sharing. Distributed key generation, threshold cryptography protocols.
Why Recommended: Extends Shamir Secret Sharing with public commitments that allow share verification — each participant can confirm their share is consistent without reconstructing the secret. Eliminates the malicious dealer problem that Shamir alone cannot address. Foundation of most threshold cryptography protocols including DKG for FROST and GG20.
Caution: Verification relies on the discrete logarithm assumption and is not post-quantum safe. Commitments reveal information about the secret (computationally hiding only, not perfectly hiding). Use Pedersen VSS for stronger hiding guarantees. Neither is quantum-safe.

Algorithm: Pedersen VSS
Category: sharing
Status: Foundational
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Information-theoretically hiding VSS. Stronger privacy than Feldman.
Why Recommended: Information-theoretically hiding verifiable secret sharing with stronger privacy than Feldman VSS — commitments are perfectly hiding. Widely used in modern threshold cryptography protocols.
Caution: Computationally binding only and relies on DLog (not PQ-safe). Requires setup parameters. Use Shamir if verifiability is not needed.

Algorithm: Additive Sharing
Category: sharing
Status: Foundational
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: MPC building block. SPDZ, ABY. n-of-n sharing for secure computation.
Why Recommended: Simplest secret sharing scheme: split secret into n random additive shares requiring all n to reconstruct (n-of-n). Foundation of many MPC protocols including SPDZ and ABY.
Caution: No threshold flexibility: all n shares required for reconstruction. Single missing share prevents recovery. Use Shamir for (t,n)-threshold requirements.

Algorithm: TFHE
Category: he
Status: Research / Zama production
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Boolean/integer FHE. Privacy-preserving ML inference, encrypted database queries, confidential computing.
Why Recommended: Fully homomorphic encryption with fast gate-level bootstrapping (~10ms). Supports arbitrary Boolean and integer computations on encrypted data. Most practical for gate-by-gate FHE evaluation.
Caution: Orders of magnitude slower than plaintext computation. Gate-by-gate evaluation limits throughput. Research-only and not suitable for latency-sensitive production workloads.

Algorithm: BGV
Category: he
Status: Research / HElib
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Batched integer FHE. Privacy-preserving statistics, aggregation, voting, exact arithmetic.
Why Recommended: Leveled homomorphic encryption optimized for exact integer and modular arithmetic. Efficient for bounded-depth circuits with SIMD batching support. Implemented in HElib.
Caution: Requires advance knowledge of circuit depth for noise management. Complex parameter selection. Research-only and practical deployment requires significant HE expertise.

Algorithm: BFV
Category: he
Status: Research / Microsoft SEAL
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Integer FHE. Similar to BGV with simpler noise management. Microsoft SEAL default.
Why Recommended: Scale-invariant variant of BGV with simpler noise management. Commonly implemented in Microsoft SEAL and other HE libraries. Suitable for integer arithmetic on encrypted data.
Caution: Multiplicative depth is bounded and not suitable for arbitrary-depth computation without bootstrapping. Research-only and parameter selection requires HE expertise.

Algorithm: CKKS
Category: he
Status: Research / widely implemented
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Approximate arithmetic FHE. Privacy-preserving ML training/inference, statistical analysis on encrypted data.
Why Recommended: Homomorphic encryption for approximate arithmetic on real numbers and floating-point data. Uniquely enables machine learning inference on encrypted data among HE schemes.
Caution: Approximate computation means results are not exact. IND-CPA-D security concern means decryption leaks noise. Not suitable when exact arithmetic or standard IND-CPA security is required.

Algorithm: Groth16 (zk-SNARK)
Category: zkp
Status: Deployed (Zcash, others)
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Succinct blockchain verification. Zcash (shielded transactions), private DeFi, credential proofs.
Why Recommended: Most widely deployed zk-SNARK with smallest proof size (~200 bytes) and fastest verification (~3ms). Requires a per-circuit trusted setup ceremony. Used in Zcash and many DeFi protocols.
Caution: Per-circuit trusted setup where compromised ceremony allows undetectable proof forgery. Not PQ-safe. Use STARKs if transparency and PQ-safety are required.

Algorithm: PLONK (zk-SNARK)
Category: zkp
Status: Widely deployed
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Universal zk-SNARK. General-purpose private smart contracts, rollups (zkSync, Aztec).
Why Recommended: Universal zk-SNARK with a single trusted setup reusable across different circuits. Proof size ~400 bytes. Widely adopted in Ethereum L2 rollups including zkSync and Polygon zkEVM.
Caution: Universal but still requires initial trusted setup. Not PQ-safe. Proof sizes larger than Groth16. Use STARKs for transparency and PQ-safety.

Algorithm: zk-STARK
Category: zkp
Status: Deployed (StarkNet, others)
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Transparent, PQ-safe zero-knowledge proofs. Blockchain scaling (StarkNet), computation integrity.
Why Recommended: Transparent zero-knowledge proof system requiring no trusted setup. Based on hash functions and polynomial IOPs. PQ-safe by construction. Strongest trust assumptions among deployed ZKPs.
Caution: Proof sizes are 100-1000x larger than Groth16 (~50-200 KB vs ~200 bytes). Verification time scales with size. Use Groth16/PLONK when proof size is critical.

Algorithm: Bulletproofs
Category: zkp
Status: Deployed (Monero, others)
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Range proofs, confidential transactions. Monero, Mimblewimble, small witness proofs.
Why Recommended: Transparent ZKP for range proofs and inner product arguments requiring no trusted setup. Used in Monero for confidential transaction amounts. Compact proofs for range statements.
Caution: Not PQ-safe and relies on DLog. Verification time linear in proof size and impractical for large circuits. Use STARKs for PQ-safe ZKPs.

Algorithm: SPDZ
Category: mpc
Status: Academic / MP-SPDZ framework
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: General-purpose malicious-secure MPC. Private auctions, secure analytics, joint ML training.
Why Recommended: Actively secure MPC protocol tolerating malicious adversaries corrupting up to n-1 of n parties. MAC-based verification ensures computation integrity even against cheating participants.
Caution: Expensive offline preprocessing phase for Beaver triple generation. Use ABY for semi-honest settings where malicious security is not required.

Algorithm: ABY
Category: mpc
Status: Academic / TU Darmstadt framework
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Two-party secure computation. Private set intersection, biometric matching, ML inference.
Why Recommended: Hybrid MPC framework combining Arithmetic, Boolean, and Yao (garbled circuit) sharing types. Efficient automatic conversion between share types enables mixed-protocol optimization.
Caution: Semi-honest security only and does not protect against actively cheating participants. Use SPDZ when malicious security is required.

Algorithm: Yao's Garbled Circuits
Category: mpc
Status: Foundational
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Two-party secure computation. Foundation for most 2-party MPC protocols.
Why Recommended: Classic two-party computation via Yao's garbled circuit construction. Enables arbitrary function evaluation where neither party reveals their input. Free XOR and half-gates optimizations make it practical for real-world 2-party computation. Foundation of the ABY framework and many commercial MPC implementations.
Caution: Communication cost proportional to circuit size — large circuits require significant bandwidth. Limited to two parties in standard form. Use secret-sharing MPC (SPDZ, ABY) for multi-party or bandwidth-constrained scenarios.

Algorithm: Sharemind
Category: mpc
Status: Commercial (Cybernetica)
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Privacy-preserving data analysis. Government statistics, tax analytics, healthcare research.
Why Recommended: Industrial MPC platform using additive 3-party secret sharing. Optimized for database operations and statistical analysis on secret-shared data with practical performance.
Caution: Fixed 3-party honest-majority model and not flexible for arbitrary party counts or adversary models. Use SPDZ for general malicious-secure MPC.

Algorithm: Base Oblivious Transfer
Category: ot_pir
Status: Foundational
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Foundation for MPC, garbled circuits, and private set intersection. Building block for higher protocols.
Why Recommended: Foundational cryptographic primitive: sender has two messages, receiver selects one without revealing the choice. Building block for garbled circuits, MPC, and zero-knowledge protocols.
Caution: Computationally expensive per instance. Standard DLog/RSA constructions are not PQ-safe. Use OT extension for bulk OT operations.

Algorithm: OT Extension
Category: ot_pir
Status: Foundational / IKNP
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Efficient bulk OT for large-scale MPC. Millions of OTs from a few base OTs.
Why Recommended: Amortizes expensive base OTs into millions of efficient OTs using only symmetric cryptography (IKNP protocol). Essential for practical MPC at scale where many OTs are needed.
Caution: Requires base OTs as setup and inherits their assumptions. Security depends on correlation robustness of the hash function.

Algorithm: Computational PIR
Category: ot_pir
Status: Research / emerging deployment
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Private database queries. DNS privacy, certificate transparency lookups, ad-tech, private search.
Why Recommended: Computational private information retrieval allowing a client to fetch a database entry without the server learning which entry was requested. Single-server model based on homomorphic encryption.
Caution: High server-side computational cost (linear HE scan). Use IT-PIR with multiple servers if non-collusion can be guaranteed.

Algorithm: Information-Theoretic PIR
Category: ot_pir
Status: Foundational
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 256
Use Cases: Multi-server PIR with unconditional privacy. Maximum privacy guarantee.
Why Recommended: Information-theoretic PIR providing unconditional privacy regardless of server computational power. Requires multiple non-colluding servers. Strongest privacy guarantee for private database retrieval.
Caution: Requires multiple non-colluding servers where any collusion breaks privacy completely. Higher communication overhead than CPIR.

Algorithm: RSA-OAEP-2048
Category: asymmetric
Status: NIST SP 800-56B Rev 2 / RFC 8017
Security Bits: 112
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Legacy public-key encryption. Encrypting small payloads (symmetric keys), S/MIME, PGP legacy, Java JAR signing.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-56B Rev 2 and RFC 8017 standardized. Provides 112-bit classical security with 2048-bit key. Acceptable for legacy compatibility but NIST has set 2030 as the deprecation horizon for 112-bit security.
Caution: 112-bit security is below the 128-bit target for long-term security. Fully broken by Shor's algorithm — a cryptographically relevant quantum computer eliminates all RSA security. Migrate to ML-KEM-768 for key encapsulation. Do not use RSA for any new system design.

Algorithm: RSA-OAEP-4096
Category: asymmetric
Status: NIST SP 800-56B Rev 2 / RFC 8017
Security Bits: 140
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Higher-security RSA encryption. Long-term key protection, document encryption beyond 2030 NIST horizon.
Why Recommended: Provides approximately 140-bit classical security — stronger than RSA-2048 and above the NIST 128-bit long-term security target. Still not post-quantum safe. Acceptable only for systems requiring RSA with an extended classical security margin where migration is not yet possible.
Caution: Fully broken by Shor's algorithm regardless of key size. The larger key provides no quantum protection and introduces significant performance overhead. Migrate to ML-KEM-768 for new designs. RSA-4096 is a stopgap, not a long-term solution.

Algorithm: ElGamal
Category: asymmetric
Status: IEEE / Academic (no current NIST standard)
Security Bits: 80
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Legacy public-key encryption. PGP v2 (historical). Foundation for DSA, Schnorr, ECIES, and subsequent DL-based schemes.
Why Recommended: Legacy discrete-log-based encryption from 1985. Semantic security proven under DDH. Modern deployments use obsolete 1024-bit groups providing only 80-bit classical security. No current NIST standard for use as an encryption scheme. Migrate to ECIES (P-256) for classical deployments or ML-KEM for post-quantum safety.
Caution: Not standardized for current use. Legacy implementations use 1024-bit groups (80-bit security, deprecated). Ciphertext is double the message size. Not PQ-safe. Probabilistic encryption requires high-quality randomness per message.

Algorithm: ECIES (P-256)
Category: asymmetric
Status: ANSI X9.63 / ISO/IEC 18033-2
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Elliptic-curve integrated encryption. TLS client-certificate encryption, secure messaging key wrapping, IoT key establishment.
Why Recommended: ANSI X9.63 and ISO/IEC 18033-2 standardized. 128-bit classical security on P-256. Acceptable for current use but not post-quantum safe. Better choice than RSA due to smaller keys and stronger security-per-bit ratio.
Caution: Not post-quantum safe — ECDLP is broken by Shor's algorithm. Migrate to ML-KEM-768 for key encapsulation. Use X25519+ML-KEM-768 hybrid as an intermediate step while ecosystem tooling catches up.

Algorithm: SM2 Encryption
Category: asymmetric
Status: GB/T 32918 / ISO/IEC 14888-3
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Chinese government and financial public-key encryption. TLS with SM certificate suite, Chinese PKI.
Why Recommended: Chinese national standard (GB/T 32918) required for government and financial systems. Equivalent security margin to ECIES (P-256) on a 256-bit elliptic curve. Registered with ISO/IEC 14888-3.
Caution: Limited deployment outside Chinese compliance contexts. Not post-quantum safe. SM2 curve parameter derivation is less publicly documented than NIST P-256.

Algorithm: LSB Substitution
Category: steganography
Status: Academic (widely analyzed)
Security Bits: 1
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 1
Use Cases: Image steganography. Historically: hiding text in BMP/PNG images by replacing least-significant bits of pixel values.
Why Recommended: Simplest steganography technique with very high detectability. RS and SPA statistical analyses reliably detect LSB embedding at any meaningful payload. No longer adequate for adversarial covert communication against modern steganalysis. Replace with WOW adaptive steganography for any application requiring steganalysis resistance.
Caution: Easily detected by RS analysis, chi-square test, and sample pair analysis (SPA). Changes the statistical distribution of natural images in measurable ways. Any competent steganalyst will find it.

Algorithm: DCT-Domain (JPEG F5)
Category: steganography
Status: Academic / widely implemented
Security Bits: 64
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 64
Use Cases: JPEG steganography with improved steganalysis resistance. Embedding in DCT coefficients using matrix encoding.
Why Recommended: F5 embeds into JPEG DCT coefficients using matrix embedding (Hamming codes) for capacity efficiency. Significantly more steganalysis-resistant than LSB for low embedding rates. Detectable by histogram-based calibration attacks at higher rates.
Caution: Detectable by histogram-based calibration analysis at higher embedding rates. Less effective on images with large uniform regions (shrinkage artifact). Superseded by adaptive cost-function algorithms.

Algorithm: BPCS (Bit-Plane Complexity Segmentation)
Category: steganography
Status: Academic
Security Bits: 64
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 64
Use Cases: High-capacity image steganography. Hides data in visually complex bit-planes where natural images are statistically noisy.
Why Recommended: Achieves much higher capacity (up to ~50% of image data) than LSB by targeting visually complex regions. Only embeds in bit-planes that exceed a complexity threshold, making statistical detection harder than LSB.
Caution: Complexity threshold selection creates a detectable boundary signature — unusual bit-plane statistics near the threshold boundary leak embedding regions. Not state-of-the-art compared to adaptive cost-function methods.

Algorithm: Spread Spectrum Watermarking
Category: steganography
Status: IEEE / Academic
Security Bits: 64
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 64
Use Cases: Digital watermarking for copyright protection. Imperceptible ownership marks in images, audio, video. Foundation of commercial watermarking (Adobe Content Credentials).
Why Recommended: Seminal spread-spectrum watermarking scheme with proven robustness to common signal processing attacks (JPEG compression, cropping, filtering, scaling). Widely used as foundation for commercial watermarking systems.
Caution: Designed for watermarking (robustness to transformation) rather than covert communication (undetectability to steganalysis). Low payload capacity. Not suitable for high-payload covert communication — use WOW instead.

Algorithm: Adaptive Steganography (WOW)
Category: steganography
Status: Academic (WIFS 2012)
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: State-of-the-art image steganography. Modern covert communication requiring resistance to trained steganalysis classifiers.
Why Recommended: State-of-the-art adaptive steganography using a wavelet-based distortion cost function. Concentrates embedding in textured, complex regions where natural images are hardest to model statistically. Significantly outperforms non-adaptive schemes against modern trained steganalysis classifiers.
Caution: Does not provide information-theoretic undetectability — only computational steganalysis resistance against current detectors. Deep-learning detectors have made advances against WOW at embedding rates above ~0.4 bpp.

Algorithm: FROST
Category: threshold_sig
Status: IETF RFC 9591
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Threshold Schnorr/EdDSA signatures. Distributed key generation, distributed signing, multi-party custody without key reconstruction.
Why Recommended: IETF-standardized (RFC 9591) threshold Schnorr signature scheme with identifiable abort. Two-round signing protocol. Strongly secure under the discrete logarithm assumption. First threshold signature scheme with a full IETF standard.
Caution: Not post-quantum safe. Two-round interactive protocol requires all T signers to be online simultaneously. Use in trusted communication channels; DL-based security broken by Shor

Algorithm: GG20
Category: threshold_sig
Status: Academic / widely deployed
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Threshold ECDSA for cryptocurrency custody. Produces standard ECDSA signatures compatible with Ethereum and Bitcoin without chain modifications.
Why Recommended: Most widely deployed threshold ECDSA scheme. Enables threshold signing producing signatures indistinguishable from standard ECDSA — no chain-level changes required. Used in major cryptocurrency custody platforms including Fireblocks, ZenGo, and others. Identifiable abort means cheating parties can be identified.
Caution: ECDSA's non-linear signing equation makes threshold construction significantly more complex than Schnorr-based schemes. Implementation errors have caused real-world vulnerabilities. Multiple rounds of interaction required. Not post-quantum safe — ECDLP broken by Shor's algorithm. Prefer FROST for threshold Schnorr where chain compatibility allows.

Algorithm: BLS Threshold
Category: threshold_sig
Status: IETF draft-irtf-cfrg-bls-signature
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Threshold signatures with non-interactive aggregation. Ethereum 2.0 validator set, DFINITY BLS, blockchain consensus protocols.
Why Recommended: Pairing-based signature scheme with unique aggregation: many signatures can be non-interactively combined into one constant-size signature. Enables O(1) verification for large validator sets. Ethereum 2.0 uses BLS threshold signatures for validator staking.
Caution: Not post-quantum safe — pairing-based group assumptions broken by quantum computers. Pairing operations are computationally expensive (~1ms per pairing). Use only where aggregation is the key value proposition.

Algorithm: Shamir+Schnorr
Category: threshold_sig
Status: Academic / ACISP 2001
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Provably secure distributed Schnorr signatures. Academic benchmark; theoretical foundation that FROST builds upon.
Why Recommended: Provably secure threshold Schnorr scheme using Pedersen DKG for distributed key generation and Shamir sharing for partial signatures. Security proven in the random oracle model by Stinson & Strobl (2001). Solid construction that established the foundations later improved by FROST.
Caution: Superseded by FROST which has an IETF standard, cleaner protocol, and better tooling. More complex to implement correctly than FROST. Prefer FROST for any new threshold Schnorr work.

Algorithm: DKLS23
Category: threshold_sig
Status: Research / IEEE S&P 2024
Security Bits: 128
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 0
Use Cases: Three-round threshold ECDSA. Next-generation replacement for GG20 with reduced round complexity.
Why Recommended: Recent academic result providing threshold ECDSA in only 3 rounds versus GG20's 8+ rounds. Tighter security proof and simpler protocol structure. Published at IEEE S&P 2024 with formal security analysis. Promising candidate for next-generation threshold ECDSA custody systems.
Caution: 2023 publication with insufficient time for broad independent cryptanalysis. No production libraries with wide ecosystem support yet. Use GG20 for current production threshold ECDSA deployments. Not post-quantum safe.

Algorithm: HMAC-DRBG
Category: csprng
Status: NIST SP 800-90A Rev 1
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: General-purpose CSPRNG. OpenSSL ≥ 1.0.2, Java SecureRandom (SUN provider), many TLS and FIPS-compliant implementations.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-90A Rev 1 standard. Provides backtracking resistance, prediction resistance, and catastrophic reseed recovery. Security proven under HMAC-SHA-256 PRF assumption. No known weaknesses — contrast with the backdoored Dual_EC_DRBG in the same document.
Caution: Slower than CTR-DRBG on AES-NI hardware. For FIPS-compliant environments, either HMAC-DRBG or CTR-DRBG is correct — choose CTR-DRBG if AES-NI throughput is critical. Note: Dual_EC_DRBG in the same NIST SP 800-90A document was backdoored; HMAC-DRBG is not affected.

Algorithm: CTR-DRBG (AES-256)
Category: csprng
Status: NIST SP 800-90A Rev 1
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: High-throughput CSPRNG on AES-NI hardware. Windows CNG (BCryptGenRandom), hardware security modules globally.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-90A Rev 1 standard hardware-accelerated via AES-NI. The fastest NIST-approved DRBG on modern CPUs with AES-256 backing providing 256-bit state security. Used in hardware security modules globally.
Caution: Requires AES-NI for best performance; software AES is subject to cache-timing attacks. ChaCha20-DRBG preferred for constant-time software environments on platforms without AES-NI.

Algorithm: Hash-DRBG
Category: csprng
Status: NIST SP 800-90A Rev 1
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: NIST-approved CSPRNG where neither AES-NI nor HMAC-specific optimization is required. Simpler reference implementation.
Why Recommended: NIST SP 800-90A Rev 1 standard. Simpler construction than HMAC-DRBG using a hash function directly. Provides security but has slightly weaker provable reduction than HMAC-DRBG. Acceptable as a NIST-approved fallback.
Caution: Weaker provable security than HMAC-DRBG — relies on ideal hash assumption rather than PRF assumption. Lacks catastrophic reseed recovery by design. HMAC-DRBG provides stronger security guarantees and is preferred.

Algorithm: ChaCha20-DRBG
Category: csprng
Status: RFC 7539 + Linux kernel (non-NIST)
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: /dev/urandom and /dev/random on Linux ≥ 5.17, OpenBSD arc4random (ChaCha20 variant), crypto.getRandomValues() in many browsers.
Why Recommended: Constant-time by construction with no lookup tables, eliminating cache-timing side-channels. Used as the primary DRBG in Linux ≥ 5.17. Faster in software than HMAC-DRBG on platforms without AES-NI. Well-analyzed ChaCha20 stream cipher foundation.
Caution: Not NIST-approved — fails FIPS 140-2/3 requirements where NIST-approved DRBGs are mandated. For FIPS compliance, use HMAC-DRBG or CTR-DRBG.

Algorithm: Fortuna
Category: csprng
Status: Ferguson & Schneier 2003 / OS standard
Security Bits: 256
Post-Quantum Security Bits: 128
Use Cases: Operating-system entropy pool architecture. /dev/random in FreeBSD and macOS, LibreSSL, Apple SecRandomCopyBytes.
Why Recommended: Designed specifically for OS-level CSPRNG with catastrophic reseed recovery as a first-class property. Uses 32 entropy pools to ensure that even after complete state compromise, re-accumulated entropy from independent sources eventually restores full security. Used in FreeBSD and macOS kernels.
Caution: More complex to implement correctly than HMAC-DRBG. Relies on AES-256 in CTR mode internally. Some implementations have had bugs in pool rotation logic. Not NIST-approved.

Project: crypto::compare
URL: https://systemslibrarian.github.io/crypto-compare/
Description: Cryptographic algorithm decision system for engineers, architects, and technical decision-makers. 97 algorithms. 17 categories. 65+ linked public demos. Sourced recommendations. Safe-usage guidance. Reference architectures. Post-quantum migration context.

What This Is:
crypto::compare is a browser-based decision-support tool for choosing cryptographic algorithms responsibly. It is designed for the point where real engineering work begins: when someone has to decide what to use for messaging, storage, authentication, signatures, or post-quantum migration, and needs something better than vague advice or a random blog post.

This project helps you:
- choose algorithms by use case rather than by hype
- compare primitives side by side using consistent fields
- understand where recommendations are strong, conditional, or uncertain
- avoid common cryptographic mistakes before they become system failures

This project does NOT:
- implement cryptographic operations
- certify systems for compliance
- replace a professional security review
- protect you from implementation bugs or operational mistakes

If You're Building X, Use This:
Secure Messaging: XChaCha20-Poly1305 + X25519/ML-KEM-768 hybrid + HKDF ratchet + strong identity signatures. AEAD for confidentiality and integrity, hybrid exchange for forward secrecy plus PQ transition, ratcheting for compartmentalization. Avoid static RSA, no ratchet, random-nonce AES-GCM at very high message volume without discipline.
Password Storage / Authentication: Argon2id with strong memory cost, unique salt, TLS in transit. Memory-hard password hashing raises attacker cost materially after database compromise. Avoid MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 alone, unsalted hashes, low-cost PBKDF2 for new systems.
File Encryption at Rest: AES-256-GCM where hardware acceleration exists, otherwise XChaCha20-Poly1305; HKDF for key separation. AEAD prevents silent tampering; per-file derivation limits blast radius. Avoid ECB, CBC without authentication, nonce reuse, same key for every purpose.
API / Web Encryption: TLS 1.3 with AES-256-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305; X25519 with hybrid PQ where available. Removes legacy negotiation problems, provides forward secrecy. Avoid TLS 1.0/1.1, RSA key exchange, skipping certificate validation.
Long-Term Sensitive Data: ML-KEM-1024 + AES-256 + conservative signature choice such as SLH-DSA for archival cases. Designed for data that may need to survive the classical-to-PQ transition. Avoid waiting for a perfect migration moment, assuming RSA/ECC is fine for multi-decade secrecy.
Digital Signatures: ML-DSA for PQ planning; Ed25519 for classical deployments where appropriate. Clear modern defaults with strong ecosystems. Avoid RSA-1024, legacy DSA, fragile ECDSA deployments with poor nonce handling.

Critical Safety Rules:
- Never reuse a nonce with AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305. Reuse can destroy confidentiality and authenticity guarantees.
- Never encrypt without authentication. Confidentiality without integrity gives attackers room to manipulate ciphertext.
- Never use fast general-purpose hashes for passwords. Password storage needs memory-hard functions like Argon2id, not SHA-256.
- Never use ECB mode. It leaks structural information about plaintext.
- Never hard-code secrets. Keys in source control or build pipelines eventually leak.
- Never roll your own crypto. Custom primitives and protocols fail in ways that are difficult to detect.
- Never disable certificate verification. That turns TLS into theater.

Key Management Basics:
- generate keys with a CSPRNG, never a general-purpose PRNG
- separate keys by purpose: encryption, MAC, signing, and derivation keys should not be interchangeable
- rotate long-lived keys on an explicit policy rather than ad hoc intuition
- keep secrets in hardware-backed stores or dedicated secret-management systems where possible
- plan for compromise: revocation, re-issuance, and blast-radius reduction matter as much as initial generation

Recommended Libraries:
- General-purpose application crypto: libsodium (NaCl-family bindings across major languages)
- TLS and transport security: BoringSSL or OpenSSL 3.x
- Rust systems: ring plus rustls where appropriate
- Post-quantum experimentation: liboqs and PQClean-derived wrappers
- Browser crypto: Web Crypto API
- Go services: crypto/* and x/crypto
- JVM / .NET ecosystems: Bouncy Castle

Reference Architectures:
Secure Messaging: Identity Keys -> Hybrid Session Setup -> HKDF / Ratchet -> AEAD Message Encryption -> Transcript / State Binding. Stack: identity signatures, hybrid key establishment, HKDF-derived message keys, AEAD for messages. Properties: confidentiality, forward secrecy, compartmentalization, authenticated peer identity.
Web API / TLS-Style Transport: ClientHello -> Certificate Validation -> Ephemeral Key Agreement -> Session Keys -> AEAD Records. Stack: TLS 1.3, ephemeral key agreement, AEAD record protection, authenticated certificates.
File Encryption System: Passphrase or Master Key -> Strong KDF -> Per-File Key -> AEAD Encryption -> Store Ciphertext + Metadata. Stack: Argon2id or master-key input, HKDF-style separation, AES-256-GCM or XChaCha20-Poly1305.
Authentication System: Password -> Argon2id -> Stored Hash + Salt + Parameters -> TLS-Protected Login -> Constant-Time Verification. Stack: Argon2id, per-user salt, TLS transport, constant-time comparison, controlled session issuance.

Recommendation Levels:
- Recommended: Strong default for new systems in the stated context
- Acceptable: Reasonable in bounded scenarios, compatibility needs, or transitional environments
- Legacy: Still encountered, but not the direction to choose for new systems
- Research: Interesting, promising, or relevant, but not a safe default for ordinary deployment
- Avoid: Unsafe, obsolete, or too failure-prone for responsible new use
